result:
The window is made of that glass that warps the world slightly, turning the neighbor’s fence into a liquid ripple of grey. It’s raining, but quietly, the kind that doesn’t so much fall as hang in the air, a shimmering veil. My coffee is no longer hot, just a tepid, bitter pool in the bottom of the cup. I think of all the things I could write with a thousand words. A manifesto, a confession, a love letter to a ghost. I could describe the precise pattern of cracks in the ceiling plaster, mapping them like rivers on a forgotten continent. I could list every book I’ve ever abandoned halfway through, a cemetery of good intentions.

Instead, I think about the weight of a thousand words. They are not all equal. Some are feathers, like “perhaps” or “murmur.” Others are stones: “terminal,” “never,” “enough.” You could build a shelter with a thousand well-chosen words, or you could start a war. Most of us use them like loose change, scattering them without hearing them jingle. We spend a hundred on describing a mediocre meal, hoard the ones that really matter until they go stale in our pockets.

The rain picks up. It drums a uneven rhythm on the sill. There is a spider in the upper corner, motionless, a perfect punctuation mark against the white. It has written its entire existence in a language of silk and patience, a single, repeated word: *wait*.

A thousand words is a space. A container. It is the length of a deep breath held, then slowly released. You could fit a childhood memory into it: the smell of your grandfather’s work jacket—oil, Old Spice, and the faint, sweet scent of hay. The shocking red of a tricycle against a green lawn. The paralyzing fear of the deep end of the pool, the chlorine burning your nostrils as you sank. You could capture a single, life-altering minute: the way the light fell across a hospital floor, the weight of a hand in yours growing lighter, the silence that wasn’t really silence but the whole world holding its breath.

You could also waste them. You could argue about politics, regurgitating slogans that are not your own. You could craft the perfect, cutting remark designed to win a pointless argument and lose a friend. You could fill the space with noise, the digital static of the age: “algorithm,” “optimize,” “content,” “engagement.” Hollow words, calories without nourishment.

I remember reading that the average person speaks around 16,000 words a day. What a torrent. What a flood. And yet, how often do we say the thing? The real thing? We use words as buffers, as moats, as dazzling distractions. We speak in clouds, not in arrows.

A thousand words is a chance to aim one arrow, true.

The rain is slowing now. The world outside the warped glass is coming back into focus, dripping and renewed. My cold coffee has a single, oily rainbow floating on its surface, a tiny, shattered prism. I think about connection. That’s what we’re all trying to do with words, isn’t it? To signal through the fog. *I am here*, we whisper into the darkness. *Are you there?* The greatest sentences ever written are just variations on that call. From “Call me Ishmael” to “In the beginning” to “Dear you, I miss you more than I can say.”

We are story-making creatures. We take the chaos of sensation and emotion—the smell of rain, the ache of loss, the warmth of a sunbeam—and we string them together on a line of words to make sense of it. To share it. A thousand words can be a lifeline, thrown across the void between one consciousness and another. Sometimes, if we are very lucky, it is caught.

I could tell you about my day, but it was unremarkable. I could invent a tale of pirates or spies, but the truth feels more urgent tonight. The truth is this: I am a person sitting in a quiet room, watching the day end. I have known joy and grief. I have been kind and I have been cruel. I am trying, most of the time, to be better. I believe in the redeeming power of a shared laugh, in the sanctity of a silent moment with someone you trust. I believe that moss growing on the north side of a stone is a quiet miracle. I am afraid of the future, but I am curious about it, too.

A thousand words are almost gone now. They slip away like the seconds, used up in the simple act of reaching out. This is the great, funny tragedy of writing, of speaking, of *trying*. You use your breath, your ink, your pixels, to build a bridge. And you stand at the edge of it, offering a hand, never knowing if anyone will cross, or if your construction will hold.

The spider has begun to move, descending on an invisible thread. It has decided the wait is over. The rain has stopped. A last droplet clings to the window, catching the burgeoning evening light, holding a whole, tiny, brilliant sky in its curve.

And that’s it. The space is filled. Not with everything, but with something. A handful of stones, a few feathers, offered from my quiet room to yours. A simple, profound message, sent with the last of the words:

*I was here. And for a moment, so were you.*

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
